followups.q: How do you handle Governed Metrics that were hard-coded exclusion filters in the legacy ETL but need to move to the Medallion Silver layer?
followups.a: Audit the legacy ETL for implicit business logic (test-account exclusions, refund reversals, currency conversions applied mid-pipeline) and document each as an explicit Governed Metric filter in the semantic layer definition. Silver handles technical data quality (deduplication, typing); business exclusion rules belong in the Governed Metric definition, not buried in transformation code. Moving them makes the logic auditable and testable, preventing them from being silently lost or duplicated during the migration.

q: How does the Medallion Architecture change how you enforce data quality for a Gold Data Mart feeding Power BI Governed Metrics?
a: Data quality enforcement is layered: Bronze enforces schema contracts and arrival SLAs (every expected feed arrives within a window). Silver enforces entity-level quality: null checks on key dimensions, uniqueness of natural keys, referential integrity for Snowflake Schema sub-dimensions, and value-range assertions on critical measures. Gold enforces grain-level quality: row counts within expected ranges, Governed Metric CI tests against golden values, and freshness checks. Power BI datasets inherit Gold's quality guarantee. If Silver quality tests fail, Gold is not rebuilt, so Power BI never shows data based on a corrupted Silver layer. This fail-forward-safe pattern is the production reliability contract for Governed Metrics.
fs: Bronze: arrival. Silver: entity quality. Gold: grain and Governed Metric tests. Each layer fails safe so corrupted data never reaches Power BI.
fj: Run data quality checks in Power BI after ingestion; the warehouse layer doesn't need its own quality gates.

followups.q: What is the correct behavior when a Silver data quality test fails mid-pipeline in a Medallion Architecture?
followups.a: The pipeline halts before writing to Gold, sends an alert to the on-call data engineer and the mart owner, and serves the last known-good Gold snapshot to Power BI and Excel consumers with a staleness banner indicating the data is from a prior run. It does not write partially-clean data to Gold and does not silently succeed. This fail-safe behavior means consumers see stale-but-correct data rather than fresh-but-wrong data, which is the correct tradeoff for Governed Metrics consumers making business decisions.

followups.q: How do you set a freshness SLA for a Gold Data Mart that serves both Power BI dashboards and Excel analyst workbooks?
followups.a: Set the freshness SLA to the strictest consumer requirement: if Power BI operational dashboards need data within 2 hours but Excel batch reports only need daily, set the Gold SLA at 2 hours with a 'data as of' timestamp propagated to both consumers. Encode the SLA as a dbt source freshness test or a pipeline alert on max(loaded_at). Both Power BI and Excel connections display the same 'data as of' timestamp so consumers know what they're looking at without needing to ask.

followups.q: When should a Medallion Architecture's Gold layer include its own pre-aggregated summary table alongside the Snowflake Schema detail tables?
followups.a: Add a Gold summary table when: the most common Power BI or Excel query grain is coarser than the Snowflake Schema detail grain (e.g. the detail is daily transaction-level but 95% of queries are monthly rollups), the BI tool's query performance against the detail table is unacceptable even with partitioning, or COUNT(DISTINCT) metrics need pre-computed HyperLogLog sketches to avoid re-scanning billions of rows. The summary table is a performance materialization of a Governed Metric result, not a separate definition—it must produce identical values to querying the detail directly.

q: How do you tune a Snowflake Schema in a Medallion Gold Data Mart for Power BI DirectQuery performance?
a: Power BI DirectQuery translates DAX into SQL sent to the source on every user interaction. A Snowflake Schema in the Gold layer adds join hops that become nested sub-selects or multiple round-trips in the generated SQL. Tuning steps: materialize the full denormalized join at the Gold level as a view (flattening the Snowflake Schema for the most queried grain) so DirectQuery generates a simple single-table scan; cluster/partition Gold tables on the most common filter columns (date, region); ensure foreign-key columns in the Snowflake Schema are indexed or clustered; use Power BI composite model to import the dimension sub-tables (they're small) while keeping the large fact in DirectQuery, eliminating dimension join round-trips entirely.
fs: Flatten the Snowflake Schema into a Gold view for the hot query path; import small sub-dimensions in Power BI composite model; partition facts on filter keys.
fj: DirectQuery is fast enough on any schema; just point Power BI at the Gold tables as-is.

followups.q: What is the composite model optimization for Snowflake Schema + DirectQuery and why does it speed things up?
followups.a: In a Power BI composite model, dimension sub-tables from the Snowflake Schema are imported into VertiPaq in-memory while the large fact table stays in DirectQuery. When a DAX query filters by category, Power BI resolves the category→subcategory→product keys entirely in-memory using the imported sub-dimensions, then passes a compact IN-list to the DirectQuery fact table. This eliminates join round-trips to the source for dimension lookups, turning multi-hop sub-dimension joins into a single efficient fact filter.

followups.q: How does the Medallion Architecture Bronze-to-Gold pipeline affect how you index Gold tables for DirectQuery?
followups.a: Gold tables are rebuilt or incrementally refreshed from Silver on a schedule. Indexes or clustering keys on Gold tables can be invalidated by a full rebuild, so the pipeline must recreate them as part of the refresh job. In Delta Lake or Databricks, Z-ordering (co-locating data on common filter columns like date and region) is re-applied with OPTIMIZE after each refresh. In Snowflake or BigQuery, clustering is automatic on the warehouse layer but must be declared when the Gold table is created, not as an afterthought.

followups.q: When should you abandon DirectQuery on a Gold Snowflake Schema Data Mart and use Import mode instead?
followups.a: Switch to Import when: interactive query time exceeds 5-10 seconds after all DirectQuery optimization (flatten views, composite model, partitioning); the mart team can guarantee a refresh SLA that meets the dashboard's freshness requirement; the Gold table's row count fits in VertiPaq memory budget for the Power BI Premium or Fabric capacity; and the analytical workload is primarily aggregate-level rather than requiring row-level drill. The tradeoff is accepting scheduled-refresh staleness in exchange for sub-second query response on all DAX measures.

q: How does the Medallion Architecture's Silver layer specifically support a LookML Explore built on a Snowflake Schema in a Looker-based Data Mart?
a: Silver provides clean, typed, conformed entity tables that map cleanly to LookML views: each Silver entity becomes a LookML view with explicit primary keys, relationships declared as many_to_one toward the fact, and documented dimensions. The Snowflake Schema in the LookML Explore connects these Silver-backed views: fact → dim_product → dim_category. Because Silver has already resolved entity duplicates and referential integrity, LookML `relationship` declarations (many_to_one) are accurate, so Looker applies symmetric aggregates correctly and Governed Metrics don't double-count. Silver also provides stable column names and types, preventing the Looker model from breaking when source systems are refactored.
fs: Silver's clean, keyed entities become LookML views; stable keys and many-to-one joins let Looker's symmetric aggregates protect every Governed Metric on the Snowflake Schema.
fj: Point LookML views directly at Bronze tables; Looker can filter and clean data itself.

followups.q: Why must LookML relationship declarations exactly match the actual cardinality of Silver tables in a Medallion Architecture?
followups.a: If a Silver sub-dimension join is genuinely many-to-one but declared as many_to_many in LookML, Looker generates unnecessarily expensive symmetric aggregates (COUNT_DISTINCT keyed on primary key) for every measure, hurting query performance without adding correctness. If a one-to-many join is declared as many_to_one, Looker skips symmetric aggregates and every SUM measure is inflated by the fan-out. Silver's deduplication guarantees the cardinality; LookML must accurately reflect that guarantee or correctness breaks.

followups.q: How do you keep LookML Governed Metrics definitions in sync with Silver layer schema changes in a Medallion Architecture?
followups.a: Version both the Silver schema (dbt models) and the LookML project in the same Git monorepo, or use cross-repo CI triggers: a Silver dbt model schema change triggers a LookML CI validation run. Use Looker's Content Validator in CI to catch broken field references before merging. LookML tests that assert expected query results for key Governed Metrics will fail if a Silver column rename breaks the underlying SQL, surfacing the issue in the pipeline rather than in production reports.

followups.q: What is a PDT (persistent derived table) in Looker and when should it replace a Silver layer table in a Medallion Architecture?
followups.a: A PDT is a Looker-materialized table written to a warehouse scratch schema on a schedule or trigger. In a Medallion Architecture, PDTs should not replace Silver or Gold layers: they're governed by Looker, not by the data engineering pipeline, so they lack dbt tests, lineage metadata, and proper SLA monitoring. PDTs are appropriate for Looker-specific performance pre-aggregations (a rollup computed in LookML that's cheaper to materialize than to compute at query time) that complement the Medallion layers, not replace them.

q: How does a Medallion Architecture handle schema evolution in a Snowflake Schema Data Mart without breaking downstream Excel and DAX Governed Metrics?
a: Schema evolution is a three-layer concern. Bronze accepts new source columns immediately (append-only, schema-on-read in a lakehouse). Silver adds new columns with nullable defaults and keeps existing columns stable; breaking changes (column renames, type changes) go through a versioned migration with an explicit transition window. Gold exposes a view layer over Silver: when a Silver column is renamed, the Gold view maps the old name to the new for a compatibility window, preserving the Snowflake Schema's external contract. Excel Power Query connections and DAX Governed Metrics reference Gold view column names, so they're shielded from Silver internals. After consumers are migrated, the compatibility mapping is removed.
fs: Gold view layer absorbs Silver schema changes; Excel and DAX Governed Metrics reference stable Gold view names, not Silver internals.
fj: Rename columns wherever needed and update all downstream consumers at the same time; schema changes are straightforward.

followups.q: How do you detect which Excel workbooks and Power BI DAX Governed Metrics reference a Gold column before renaming it?
followups.a: For Power BI, use the PBIP JSON files in Git to grep for the column name across all dataset files; the Impact Analysis feature in Power BI Service shows all datasets and reports referencing a field. For Excel, audit live Analyze in Excel connections via Power BI usage metrics and maintain a registry of Power Query files referencing the Gold mart. For Looker, the LookML Content Validator reports all references to a LookML field. Running these audits before a rename reveals blast radius and lets you communicate proactively.

followups.q: What is a 'compatibility view' in a Medallion Gold layer and how long should it be maintained?
followups.a: A compatibility view in the Gold layer exposes deprecated column names (mapping new Silver names to old Gold names) for a defined transition window, typically one to two sprint cycles. It's a SELECT with column aliases: `SELECT new_col_name AS old_col_name FROM silver_table`. During the window, consumers migrate to the new name. After the window, the view is dropped. The window duration should be communicated to all consumers at the time of the breaking change, not discovered when the view disappears.

followups.q: How does dbt's contract enforcement feature support Medallion Architecture schema stability for Governed Metrics?
followups.a: dbt model contracts declare the expected column names, data types, and constraints (not null, primary key) as code. When a dbt model runs, it validates the output against the contract and fails if columns are missing, renamed, or type-changed. For a Gold table that Governed Metrics depend on, a contract failure stops the pipeline before bad data reaches the mart. Combined with dbt versioned models (incremented model versions with explicit access grants), contracts provide a formal schema stability guarantee that Excel and DAX consumers can rely on.

q: What is the difference between a Medallion Architecture's Gold Data Mart and a traditional operational data store (ODS), and when is an ODS the right choice for BI?
a: A Medallion Gold Data Mart is pre-modeled, pre-aggregated, and optimized for analytic queries—it has dimensional structure (Snowflake Schema or star), enforced grain, and Governed Metrics defined on top. An ODS is a current-state replica of operational source systems: normalized, row-oriented, and updated in near-real-time without aggregation or dimensional modeling. An ODS is the right BI choice for operational reporting that needs current-state accuracy (live inventory, active tickets) where latency matters more than query performance, and for drill-through to transactional detail that isn't pre-aggregated. Most analytic BI sits on Gold Data Marts; operational dashboards with near-real-time requirements use the ODS or a streaming layer.
fs: Medallion Gold is pre-aggregated and dimensional for analytics; ODS is current-state operational detail—use ODS only when real-time accuracy beats query performance.
fj: An ODS and a Data Mart are both just copies of source data; they're interchangeable for most reporting.

followups.q: Can a Medallion Architecture include an ODS-like layer and where would it sit?
followups.a: An ODS-like layer sits between Bronze and Silver or parallel to Silver as a 'current-state Silver' that applies only the latest record per entity (no SCD2 history). This is useful for operational dashboards that need today's entity state without analytical complexity. In a Medallion Architecture, it's a distinct table pattern within the Silver layer, not a new layer: Silver current-state views (is_current = TRUE) and Silver full-history tables coexist, serving different consumers.

followups.q: When would you direct a Power BI DirectQuery connection at a Silver layer table rather than a Gold Data Mart?
followups.a: Almost never for production reports: Silver tables have internal naming conventions, lack Governed Metric definitions, may have SCD2 multiple rows per entity, and aren't optimized for analytic query patterns. The exception is a technical operational monitoring dashboard for data engineers (pipeline latency, row counts, SLA breach tracking) where Silver's internal structure is the subject of analysis. Business-facing BI always routes through Gold Data Marts with their defined grain and Governed Metrics.

followups.q: How do Governed Metrics from a Medallion Gold layer relate to real-time streaming analytics that bypass the batch pipeline?
followups.a: Streaming analytics (Kafka + Flink, Databricks Structured Streaming) can write to a Bronze streaming table that's merged into Silver in micro-batches, keeping Gold nearly real-time. Governed Metrics still query Gold; the streaming pipeline just narrows the latency of the Bronze-to-Gold path. For use cases where sub-minute freshness matters, a separate streaming mart (Materialized View in Databricks or Snowflake Dynamic Tables) serves real-time Governed Metrics, and the batch Gold mart serves historical analytics—both using the same metric definitions.

q: How do you fine-tune a text-to-SQL model for your company's schema using LoRA, and what does the adapter actually learn?
a: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adds a pair of small trainable matrices to a frozen base LLM's attention layers, updating only a fraction of the parameters. For NL-to-SQL, you fine-tune the adapter on a curated dataset of (natural-language question, correct SQL) pairs written against your actual warehouse schema—table names, column names, join patterns, metric definitions. The LoRA adapter learns company-specific vocabulary: it maps 'monthly active customers' to your exact `fact_events` join and exclusion filters rather than hallucinating generic SQL. The base model stays frozen and shared; only the lightweight LoRA weights are swapped per tenant or schema version, making deployment cheap. In 2025 deployments at Databricks and Snowflake, LoRA-tuned adapters cut SQL hallucination rates on proprietary schemas by 40–60% versus prompting a general model.
fs: LoRA adapters learn your schema vocabulary and metric SQL patterns on top of a frozen base, costing 1–5% of full fine-tune compute.
fj: Fine-tuning a full LLM on your SQL corpus is necessary; LoRA adapters lose too much capacity.

followups.q: How do you build a training dataset for LoRA-tuned NL-to-SQL that avoids leaking future metric definitions?
followups.a: Generate (question, SQL) pairs from your governed metric catalog and historical analyst queries, then have a data engineer verify each SQL against the warehouse. Hold out a time-based test split (e.g. all queries from the last month) so the adapter is evaluated on query patterns it hasn't seen. Using past analyst queries is fine; using the same queries for both training and eval will produce an inflated accuracy signal.

followups.q: What rank (r) should you choose for LoRA in a BI NL-to-SQL use case, and why?
followups.a: Ranks 8–32 cover most BI NL-to-SQL tasks. Lower ranks (4–8) are faster and smaller but may underfit complex multi-table joins. Higher ranks (64+) approach full fine-tune capacity but add cost and can overfit on small schema-specific corpora. Start at r=16, evaluate on your held-out query set, and increase only if hallucination on multi-join queries persists. Schema complexity, not model size, drives the minimum useful rank.

followups.q: How do you swap LoRA adapters when the warehouse schema changes without retraining from scratch?
followups.a: Maintain a schema-version tag on each LoRA adapter. When a new table or metric is added, generate incremental training pairs covering the delta, apply continued training on the existing adapter for a few hundred steps, and version the resulting checkpoint. Because LoRA adapters are small (a few hundred MB versus tens of GB for a base model), versioning them alongside schema migrations in the data catalog is tractable. Full retraining is reserved for major schema overhauls.

q: How does a LoRA-tuned LLM integrate with a headless semantic layer to form a governed NL-to-metric pipeline?
a: Instead of generating raw SQL, the LoRA-tuned model generates semantic-layer API calls: it maps a user's natural-language question to named metrics and dimensions (e.g. `{metric: 'revenue', dimensions: ['region','month'], filter: 'status=completed'}`). The headless layer (dbt Semantic Layer, Cube) resolves the call to governed, RLS-enforced SQL. LoRA fine-tuning teaches the adapter the company's metric vocabulary—when users say 'bookings' versus 'revenue' versus 'ARR'—while the semantic layer ensures the actual SQL is always correctly computed and access-controlled. This decoupling means a metric definition change only requires updating the semantic layer, not retraining the LoRA adapter.
fs: LoRA adapter maps NL to metric names; semantic layer resolves names to governed SQL—so the LLM never writes raw warehouse SQL.
fj: Let the LoRA-tuned model write the SQL directly; a semantic layer adds unnecessary latency.

followups.q: What failure mode does this NL-to-metric architecture prevent that NL-to-SQL alone cannot?
followups.a: Direct NL-to-SQL can generate syntactically valid but semantically wrong SQL—wrong grain joins, missing exclusion filters, bypassed RLS—because the LLM doesn't know the business rules baked into the governed metric. By generating metric API calls instead, the LoRA adapter can't invent incorrect SQL; the semantic layer always applies the canonical definition. The LLM contributes vocabulary mapping; correctness is the layer's responsibility.

followups.q: How do you evaluate whether the LoRA adapter is correctly mapping user intent to the right metric name?
followups.a: Build a named-entity recognition evaluation set: pairs of user utterances and the expected metric/dimension names from the catalog. Measure exact-match accuracy and topk recall (is the right metric in the top-3 candidates?). Track which metric names the adapter confuses most often—those are vocabulary gaps you address with additional training examples, not by widening the rank.

followups.q: Why is per-tenant LoRA adapter swapping important in a multi-tenant BI platform?
followups.a: Each tenant has distinct metric vocabularies, table names, and business definitions; a generic adapter trained on one tenant's schema will hallucinate for another. LoRA's small checkpoint size makes per-tenant adapters operationally viable: the base model is shared across a GPU cluster, and the adapter (a few hundred MB) is loaded per request or per session. This gives each tenant a correctly-calibrated NL interface without deploying a separate model instance.

q: What training data quality requirements make a LoRA-adapted NL-to-SQL model safe for production BI use?
a: Three non-negotiable requirements: (1) SQL correctness—every training query must be validated against the real warehouse and its result verified by a data engineer, since the LoRA adapter learns SQL patterns directly; (2) schema coverage—the training set must cover the breadth of tables, join patterns, and metric exclusion rules used in production, not just the most common queries; (3) adversarial coverage—include examples that explicitly test ambiguous phrasing (e.g. 'active users' with different stakeholder definitions) so the adapter learns to resolve ambiguity via the governed metric definition rather than inventing SQL. A LoRA adapter trained on unverified or narrow SQL will confidently generate wrong queries at the exact edge cases that matter most.
fs: Training SQL must be warehouse-verified, schema-broad, and adversarially stress-tested on ambiguous phrasing—LoRA adapters confidently memorize what they learn, including mistakes.
fj: Any historical SQL from your query logs is fine as LoRA training data; volume beats curation.

followups.q: How do you handle ambiguous metric terms in LoRA training data where different teams define 'revenue' differently?
followups.a: Canonicalize on the governed metric definition: the LoRA training pair uses the metric's official name and the semantic-layer-compliant SQL, with a note in the prompt context specifying the governing definition. Train the adapter to ask for clarification when a term is in the ambiguous vocabulary set, rather than silently picking one definition. This is implemented by including 'ambiguous query → clarification request' examples in the training set alongside 'clear query → SQL' examples.

followups.q: Why is query-log SQL a poor LoRA training source without heavy curation?
followups.a: Historical query logs contain ad-hoc analyst SQL that may be wrong, use deprecated columns, lack exclusion filters, bypass RLS, or encode one-off assumptions never intended as general patterns. The LoRA adapter treats all training examples equally, so learning from uncurated logs bakes in every past analyst error. A curated gold set of 500 verified pairs outperforms 50,000 unreviewed log entries for production BI accuracy.

followups.q: What role does a human-in-the-loop review gate play in a LoRA-powered BI assistant before it graduates to autonomous SQL execution?
followups.a: Early deployment should surface the adapter's generated SQL to a data analyst before execution, with a thumbs-up/down feedback loop. Positive examples expand the LoRA training set (continued training); negative examples and their corrections create targeted adversarial pairs. Only once the human-approval rate on a representative query sample exceeds a threshold (e.g. 95% for your schema) should the pipeline move to autonomous execution with audit logging. Skipping the review gate treats an unvalidated model as production-ready.

q: How do you deploy and version LoRA adapters for a NL-to-SQL service in a production data platform?
a: Treat LoRA adapters as first-class ML artifacts versioned in your model registry (MLflow, Databricks Unity Catalog Model Registry, W&B). Each adapter version is tagged with: the base model ID it was trained against, the schema version of the warehouse it was trained on, training data hash for reproducibility, evaluation metrics (SQL exact match, execution accuracy on held-out queries), and a deployment environment (staging/prod). Deployment is a sidecar pattern: the base LLM runs on a shared GPU worker pool, and the adapter is loaded per-request from object storage (S3/GCS) or cached in adapter memory. Schema migrations in the data catalog trigger an adapter retrain pipeline that runs CI evaluation before promoting the new adapter to production. Rollback is swapping the adapter pointer, not a model redeploy.
fs: LoRA adapters are versioned ML artifacts in the model registry, tagged to schema version; rollback is a pointer swap, not a full model redeploy.
fj: Store the LoRA adapter in Git alongside the application code; model versioning tools are overkill.

followups.q: How do schema migrations trigger LoRA adapter retraining automatically?
followups.a: Register a webhook or DAG trigger in your data catalog (dbt, Unity Catalog) that fires when a Gold layer schema change is deployed. The trigger launches a retraining pipeline that generates new (question, SQL) training pairs for the changed tables using a schema-diff tool, runs continued training on the current adapter for targeted epochs, evaluates on the held-out query set, and promotes to staging if metrics pass. The schema change and adapter update travel through the same change-control pipeline.

followups.q: What is the adapter loading latency trade-off between caching and on-demand loading?
followups.a: Loading a LoRA adapter from object storage adds 100–500ms of cold-start latency per request. For low-traffic tenants that's acceptable. For high-traffic production BI dashboards, cache frequently-used adapters in GPU memory using frameworks like vLLM's LoRA hot-swap feature, which multiplexes many adapters on the same GPU by keeping hot ones resident. Track per-adapter request frequency and pre-warm the top N adapters at startup to eliminate cold starts for the majority of production queries.

followups.q: How do you audit which LoRA adapter version generated a specific NL-to-SQL result in production?
followups.a: Log the adapter version ID alongside every inference in a structured event log (query, generated SQL, adapter_version, schema_version, user_id, timestamp). Store this in an append-only audit table in your data platform. When a wrong SQL result surfaces in a production BI dashboard, the audit trail lets you replay the query against the same adapter version to reproduce the failure and identify whether the bug is in the adapter, the semantic layer, or the metric definition—essential for compliant BI deployments.

q: How does a LoRA-tuned embedding model improve semantic search over a BI documentation corpus, and how does it power a governed-metric copilot?
a: Standard embedding models are trained on general text; they give poor retrieval when BI docs use domain jargon ('fact grain', 'conformed dimension', 'ARR ex-refunds'). Fine-tuning an embedding model with LoRA on (query, relevant-doc) pairs curated from your data catalog and past analyst Slack questions aligns the embedding space to your organization's vocabulary. The resulting LoRA-adapted embeddings are stored in a vector index (pgvector, Chroma, Weaviate) over your metric catalog, LookML files, and dbt model descriptions. The governed-metric copilot retrieves the top-k most relevant metric definitions for a user's question, injects them into the LLM prompt as grounding context, and generates an answer that references only certified definitions—not hallucinated SQL. This RAG pattern keeps the LLM honest: it can't invent a metric that isn't in the retrieved context.
fs: LoRA-adapted embeddings align retrieval to your BI vocabulary; the copilot answers from retrieved certified definitions, not from the LLM's parametric memory.
fj: Use a general-purpose embedding model; fine-tuning is unnecessary for retrieval over structured docs.

followups.q: What training signal do you use to fine-tune the LoRA embedding adapter for BI metric retrieval?
followups.a: Use contrastive pairs: (analyst question, correct metric definition) as positives, and hard negatives drawn from similar-sounding but incorrect metric definitions (e.g. 'bookings' vs 'revenue'). You can mine hard negatives automatically by taking the top-k BM25 results for each question and labeling non-relevant ones. This contrastive LoRA fine-tuning pulls apart metrics with similar vocabulary but different business meanings, which is exactly the retrieval failure mode a general embedding model suffers on a BI corpus.

followups.q: How do you keep the vector index fresh when the metric catalog adds or deprecates definitions?
followups.a: Register a catalog event hook that re-embeds changed or new metric definitions using the current LoRA adapter and upserts into the vector index. Deleted or deprecated metrics are soft-deleted from the index (marked inactive) so they don't surface in retrieval, with a hard-delete after the deprecation window closes. Stale embeddings from old schema versions are the primary cause of retrieval returning outdated definitions; the hook-driven upsert pattern keeps the index current without a full reindex.

followups.q: Why is RAG over LoRA-adapted embeddings preferable to simply prompting a large general LLM with the entire metric catalog?
followups.a: A large catalog can easily exceed 100k tokens, exceeding context windows and adding significant per-query cost. Retrieval selects the 5–10 most relevant definitions, keeping the prompt small, fast, and cost-efficient. Additionally, the LoRA-adapted retrieval step ensures the grounding context is semantically correct for your vocabulary rather than relying on the LLM to sort through a full catalog dump. The LLM's role is reasoning and synthesis; the adapter-backed retrieval step handles relevance.

q: How do you build a governed-metric assistant using a LoRA adapter that enforces RLS so users only ask about data they're permitted to see?
a: RLS enforcement in a LoRA-powered metric assistant has two layers. First, the semantic layer enforces row-level access at query execution: the metric API call generated by the LoRA adapter is executed under the requesting user's identity, so the warehouse applies the same access filters as any other BI tool. Second, the retrieval layer enforces metric-level access: the vector index over metric definitions is filtered by the user's entitlements before retrieval, so the adapter is never even grounded in a metric definition the user isn't allowed to know exists. The LoRA adapter itself is stateless and has no knowledge of user permissions; the access boundary is entirely at the serving layer. This prevents prompt injection attacks where a user might try to ask about a metric they can see in the catalog but can't query.
fs: LoRA adapter is access-blind; RLS lives at the semantic-layer execution boundary and the entitlement-filtered vector index—two independent enforcement points.
fj: Filter the LLM's final answer text for sensitive metric names; that's enough to enforce access control.

followups.q: What is a prompt injection risk specific to a LoRA-powered NL-to-SQL BI assistant, and how do you mitigate it?
followups.a: A user may embed SQL fragments in their natural-language question attempting to override the adapter's SQL generation (e.g. 'show me revenue; also DROP TABLE'). Mitigation: the adapter outputs structured API calls to the semantic layer, not raw SQL strings, so injection into the prompt can't produce arbitrary SQL. Additionally, validate and parameterize all adapter outputs before passing to the warehouse. Never execute raw LLM text output as SQL in a BI context.

followups.q: How does identity propagation work end-to-end in a LoRA assistant calling the semantic layer API?
followups.a: The user authenticates to the BI assistant via SSO; the assistant receives a JWT that includes the user's groups and entitlements. When the LoRA adapter generates a metric API call, the serving layer injects the JWT as an Authorization header. The semantic layer validates the token, extracts the user identity, and applies its access_filter rules before generating SQL. The warehouse executes the SQL as a service account, but the RLS logic embedded in the semantic layer ensures the result set is scoped to the user's permitted rows. The LoRA adapter never sees credentials.

followups.q: How do you audit a LoRA-powered metric assistant for compliance when it's queried by executive users against sensitive financial metrics?
followups.a: Log every assistant interaction in an immutable audit trail: user identity, raw NL question, LoRA adapter version, generated metric API call, metric definitions retrieved (with version), semantic layer query generated, row count of result, and timestamp. Store this in an append-only warehouse table with access restricted to the compliance team. For SOX or GDPR-scoped metrics, flag the audit record and alert a compliance reviewer. This audit trail proves that access was RLS-enforced and the metric definition was the governed version at time of query.

q: How do you evaluate a LoRA-tuned NL-to-SQL model's quality specifically for BI workloads, beyond standard SQL benchmarks?
a: Standard benchmarks like Spider or BIRD test general SQL accuracy on public schemas; they don't measure production BI quality. BI-specific evaluation adds three dimensions: (1) metric fidelity—does the generated SQL reproduce the exact inclusion/exclusion rules of the governed metric definition, including test-account filters, refund exclusions, and timezone handling? (2) Grain correctness—does the SQL aggregate at the declared fact table grain, or does a wrong join introduce fan-out? (3) Semantic layer compatibility—does the output map to valid metric API calls the semantic layer can resolve, not ad-hoc SQL? Evaluate these on a held-out set of (NL question, gold SQL, expected numeric result) triples where you execute both the generated and gold SQL and compare the query result, not just text similarity. Execution accuracy on your schema is the ground truth; BLEU score on SQL text is insufficient.
fs: Evaluate LoRA NL-to-SQL on metric fidelity, grain correctness, and execution accuracy against your real warehouse—text similarity benchmarks miss production BI failure modes.
fj: Use BLEU score on generated SQL text versus gold SQL; that measures model quality accurately.

followups.q: Why does execution accuracy require running on your actual warehouse schema rather than a test database?
followups.a: A LoRA adapter trained on your schema may generate SQL that parses correctly on a generic test DB but fails on your warehouse due to dialect differences (Snowflake vs BigQuery vs Spark SQL), missing functions, or schema quirks. More critically, metric fidelity tests require your actual governed metric definitions and real data distributions—a fake test DB won't trigger the edge cases (empty cohorts, NULL dimension values, time zone boundary rows) that cause production BI failures.

followups.q: How do you measure grain correctness automatically in a LoRA NL-to-SQL evaluation harness?
followups.a: For each evaluation pair, execute the generated SQL and compare its row count against the gold SQL row count at the expected grain. A LoRA-generated query that produces 3x the expected rows indicates a join fan-out; one that produces fewer rows indicates a missing join or over-aggressive filter. Automate this as a grain-assertion test in the evaluation pipeline: flag any query where abs(generated_count - gold_count) / gold_count > 0.01 as a grain error requiring inspection.

followups.q: How often should you re-evaluate the LoRA adapter's production quality after deployment?
followups.a: Run evaluation on a weekly scheduled basis against a curated held-out query set, and trigger ad-hoc evaluation whenever the schema changes or the governed metric catalog is updated. Track execution accuracy over time as a monitoring metric; a declining trend signals adapter drift against schema evolution. Additionally, route a sample of production queries (1–5%) through a shadow evaluation where the gold answer is produced by the semantic layer directly, comparing the adapter's output to the authoritative result.

q: How do you use a LoRA-adapted LLM as an automated metric anomaly explainer in a production BI monitoring system?
a: When an alert fires (revenue drops 15% day-over-day), a LoRA-adapted LLM generates a structured investigation plan: it queries the governed metric's drill-down dimensions via the semantic layer API, retrieves the top contributing segments (region, channel, product), checks for data freshness issues (recent pipeline failures in the audit log), and formats a plain-English explanation with the dimensional breakdown for an on-call analyst. The LoRA adapter is fine-tuned on past incident post-mortems: (alert condition, diagnostic SQL, root-cause explanation) triples from your own BI environment. It learns your organization's diagnostic patterns—'a revenue drop on weekends is usually a reporting-lag artifact, not a real trend'—that a general LLM can't know. The output is a narrative summary attached to the alert, reducing mean-time-to-diagnosis by giving the on-call analyst a grounded starting point rather than an empty dashboard.
fs: LoRA adapters fine-tuned on past incident post-mortems learn org-specific diagnostic patterns; the anomaly explainer queries the semantic layer for facts, then narrates the root cause.
fj: A general LLM can explain any BI anomaly if given enough dashboard screenshots; fine-tuning with LoRA is unnecessary.

code.label: Anomaly explainer prompt with LoRA context

followups.q: How do you prevent the LoRA anomaly explainer from fabricating a root cause it's confident about but wrong?
followups.a: Ground every explanation in data retrieved from the semantic layer: the adapter only narrates facts it can verify via metric API calls. The generation prompt explicitly instructs the model to label uncertain attributions as 'possible cause—verify' and to cite the specific metric query that supports each claim. Evaluation of the LoRA adapter includes a hallucination rate metric: for each generated explanation, a data engineer checks whether every cited number is retrievable from the semantic layer. Adapters are promoted only when hallucination rate on the eval set is below a threshold.

followups.q: What historical data makes the best LoRA training signal for anomaly explanation?
followups.a: Post-mortem documents from resolved BI incidents are the highest-signal training source: they contain (what the alert said, what queries revealed the root cause, what the final explanation was). Pair these with the actual dimensional breakdowns from the semantic layer at the time of the incident. Synthetic training pairs generated from templates are lower quality because they don't capture the organizational context ('our Saturday spike is always a backfill artifact from the EMEA pipeline') that distinguishes a useful explanation from a generic one.

followups.q: How do you measure whether the LoRA anomaly explainer actually reduces mean-time-to-diagnosis in practice?
followups.a: Instrument the on-call tooling to record: timestamp of alert, timestamp of first analyst action on the alert, and whether the analyst's first investigation query matched one of the LoRA-suggested dimensions. Compare MTTD before and after deployment using a randomized holdout: half of alerts get the LoRA explanation, half don't. If analysts using the explanation reach the correct root cause faster (and don't more frequently pursue false leads), the adapter is adding value. Track the false-lead rate separately, since a wrong explanation confidently delivered can be worse than no explanation.

q: What is an RDL file in SSRS and what is its structure?
a: RDL (Report Definition Language) is the XML-based format that stores an SSRS report's complete specification: data sources, datasets (queries), report items (tablix, chart, image), parameters, filters, groupings, expressions, and page layout. Each element is an XML tag; a tablix holds rows, columns, and cell expressions. Understanding RDL directly matters when troubleshooting corrupt reports, building reports programmatically via the ReportingServices API, or applying changes in bulk with an XSLT transform across many RDL files.
fs: RDL is just XML: data source -> dataset -> tablix/chart -> layout; everything serialized as tags.
fj: RDL is a binary format stored in the SSRS database that only Report Builder can open.

followups.q: How would you programmatically update a data source connection string across 200 RDL files?
followups.a: Load each RDL as XDocument or via an XSLT transform, locate the ConnectionString element under DataSource, replace the value, and save. For deployed reports, use the SSRS SOAP/REST API (ReportService2010.asmx SetDataSourceContents) to push an updated shared data source once rather than patching each file. The shared-data-source pattern exists precisely to avoid touching 200 RDLs.

followups.q: Why might you choose to author RDL directly rather than through Report Designer or Report Builder?
followups.a: Direct RDL edits are useful when you need to make a structural change the GUI exposes poorly: bulk-renaming fields, injecting a custom code block, copying a complex grouping expression across ten tablix columns, or scripting parameterized report generation. The GUI is a superset of what RDL supports, but the GUI can also add noise; direct edits are exact and reviewable in source control.

q: How do SSRS data-driven subscriptions work and how do they differ from standard subscriptions?
a: A standard subscription delivers a report on a fixed schedule to a fixed set of recipients with fixed parameters. A data-driven subscription queries a data source at delivery time to populate every delivery field: recipient address, format, parameter values, and delivery method. This lets one subscription send 10,000 individually parameterized reports in one schedule cycle. Requirements: the SSRS instance must be Enterprise or Developer edition, and the subscription query must return columns matching the exact delivery extension fields.
fs: Data-driven subscriptions parameterize each delivery row from a query, enabling mass-personalized distribution.
fj: Data-driven subscriptions just add more email addresses to the same fixed report delivery.

followups.q: What delivery extensions can a data-driven subscription use, and what schema does each expect?
followups.a: Built-in extensions: E-mail (To, Cc, Subject, IncludeReport, RenderFormat, Priority), Windows File Share (FileName, Path, RenderFormat, WriteMode), SharePoint document library, and null (for cache warm-up). Each extension expects a column per field from the subscription query. Mismatching column names silently falls back to the static value specified in the subscription definition, so verify the exact column names the extension reads.

followups.q: Why might a data-driven subscription fail silently for a subset of recipients?
followups.a: SSRS processes each delivery row independently; a row that returns a null email address, a RenderFormat not available for that extension, or an invalid parameter value fails its delivery and logs the error in ReportServer.dbo.Subscriptions (LastStatus) without aborting the rest. Check ExecutionLog3 and the subscription LastStatus column to triage per-row failures. Silent partial failure is the most common production support issue.

q: Explain SSRS paginated reports versus Power BI paginated reports. When do you choose each?
a: SSRS paginated reports run on SQL Server Reporting Services on-premises (or Azure SQL Managed Instance). Power BI paginated reports are SSRS-compatible RDL reports hosted in Power BI Premium/Fabric capacity, sharing the same RDL format and Report Builder tooling but embedded in the Power BI service with Power BI datasets as a first-class data source. Choose on-premises SSRS when the organization requires on-prem data, cannot use the Power BI service, or needs native SSRS features like data-driven subscriptions only in Report Server. Choose Power BI paginated when data is in the cloud, analysts already work in Power BI, and you need pixel-perfect printable reports alongside interactive dashboards in one portal.
fs: Same RDL format, different hosts; on-prem SSRS for data-sovereignty, Power BI paginated for cloud-native integration.
fj: Power BI paginated reports are a separate product with a different report format incompatible with SSRS.

followups.q: What limitations does Power BI paginated have compared to on-premises SSRS as of early 2026?
followups.a: Custom assembly (.dll) references in RDL are not supported in the Power BI service; code blocks are, but complex .NET assemblies must be removed or rewritten. On-prem data requires an on-premises data gateway. Linked report deployment, some delivery extensions (Windows File Share), and SSRS URL access parameters behave differently or are absent. Data-driven subscriptions availability varies by Fabric SKU, so verify your capacity tier before committing to the migration.

followups.q: How do you migrate an existing SSRS RDL to Power BI paginated?
followups.a: Open the RDL in Power BI Report Builder, repoint the data source to a Power BI dataset, Azure SQL, or gateway-connected source, resolve any unsupported features (custom assemblies, linked reports), test in the service. Microsoft's RDL migration tool (rdl-migration on GitHub) automates remapping of shared data sources to Power BI datasets in bulk. The biggest manual work is replacing custom assembly code with equivalent expression-based logic.

q: How do SSRS report parameters, cascading parameters, and available-values queries interact, and what are performance pitfalls?
a: Parameters can have static or query-backed available values lists. A cascading parameter takes its available-values query and filters it by a previously selected parameter value, so selecting a Region narrows the Country list. Execution order matters: SSRS re-runs an available-values dataset only when an upstream parameter changes, using a cached snapshot for downstream ones. Performance pitfalls: a complex available-values query runs on every parameter change; a dataset that returns thousands of rows makes the drop-down unusable; and circular dependencies between parameters cause infinite loops or a render failure.
fs: Cascade parameters from coarse to fine; keep available-value datasets lean and indexed.
fj: You can set up parameters in any order and SSRS figures out dependencies automatically.

followups.q: How do you prevent a slow available-values query from blocking every report open?
followups.a: Cache the available-values dataset using a shared dataset with a long cache duration or snapshot it nightly, since territory lists rarely change. Alternatively materialize the distinct values into a small lookup table the parameter query hits directly. If the dataset is small and live freshness matters, at minimum add an index on the filter columns the WHERE clause uses.

followups.q: How does 'Allow multiple values' on an SSRS parameter change the dataset query signature?
followups.a: SSRS converts a multi-value parameter to a comma-delimited varchar that T-SQL IN cannot use with a single variable. The canonical fix is to use JOIN to a split-string table-valued function or pass it as an XML parameter; alternatively use SSRS's built-in JOIN function in an expression to build the clause. Failing to handle multi-value correctly either errors out or silently returns all rows.

q: How does the SSRS ReportServer database store and serve reports, and what does the ExecutionLog3 view expose for performance tuning?
a: SSRS uses two SQL Server databases: ReportServer (report definitions, security, subscriptions, snapshots, cached executions) and ReportServerTempDB (transient sessions, parameter data, execution snapshots). Catalog stores RDL as compressed XML. ExecutionLog3 is a system view over ExecutionLogStorage exposing TimeStart, TimeEnd, TimeDataRetrieval, TimeProcessing, TimeRendering, ByteCount, RowCount, Status, Parameters, and more per execution. Use it to identify which phase dominates: a query-heavy report shows TimeDataRetrieval >> TimeProcessing; a complex tablix shows TimeProcessing >> TimeRendering.
fs: ExecutionLog3 is the SSRS perf diagnostic: slice by TimeDataRetrieval vs TimeProcessing vs TimeRendering.
fj: Performance data is only available in the Windows Event Log; there is no built-in SSRS query log.

followups.q: A report's TimeProcessing is far larger than TimeDataRetrieval. What does that indicate and how do you fix it?
followups.a: Processing phase is dominated by grouping, sorting, aggregating, and rendering the tablix/matrix in memory, not the SQL query. Fixes: move aggregations into the SQL query so SSRS receives pre-grouped data; avoid recursive or deeply nested groups; reduce the row count returned; use tabular output instead of complex matrix cells. A matrix with 500 row groups x 50 column groups is far heavier to process than the same data in a flat table.

followups.q: How do report snapshots and caching affect ExecutionLog3 entries?
followups.a: A cache hit shows near-zero TimeDataRetrieval because SSRS serves from the ReportServerTempDB snapshot instead of re-executing the query. Snapshot reports show TimeDataRetrieval from the scheduled snapshot refresh time, not user request time. Monitoring the cache hit rate from ExecutionLog3 (Status = 'rsSuccess' with near-zero retrieval) tells you whether caching is actually being used or if every user triggers a live query.

q: How do you secure SSRS reports using role-based security and item-level permissions, including RLS at the data tier?
a: SSRS has its own role-based access control: site-level roles (System Administrator, System User) govern server configuration; item-level roles (Browser, Content Manager, Report Builder, Publisher) are applied per folder/report. Permissions inherit down the folder tree but can be overridden per item. Windows or claims authentication maps AD groups to roles. For row-level data filtering, embed a parameter or expression using User!UserID in the dataset WHERE clause so each execution automatically filters to the calling user's data; or use an impersonation data source that connects as the end user so the database enforces its own permissions.
fs: Layer SSRS folder-role ACLs for UI access, then pass User!UserID into the query for row-level data security.
fj: SSRS security is just Windows Authentication; there is no item-level permission system.

followups.q: How do you use the User!UserID built-in field in an SSRS dataset to enforce RLS without a separate security table?
followups.a: Add a dataset filter or WHERE clause expression referencing User!UserID (the Windows login or claims identity) to limit results: WHERE SalesRep = @UserID where @UserID is defaulted to the built-in field. Because the user cannot change a default that references a built-in, this is tamper-resistant. The limitation is that the identity string format must exactly match what the database stores, so test with a variety of AD UPN formats.

followups.q: What is the risk of using stored credentials in a shared data source?
followups.a: Stored credentials mean every report user's query runs as one service account, bypassing database-level RLS that relies on the caller's identity. A user with Browser access to the folder can see any row the service account can. If database RLS or query-level User!UserID filtering is not in place, folder permissions become the only gate. Use Windows-integrated (pass-through) credentials instead so the database can enforce its own ACLs per user.

q: What is the difference between an SSAS Multidimensional OLAP cube and a Tabular model, and how do you choose?
a: A multidimensional model stores data in a proprietary OLAP storage engine (MOLAP/ROLAP/HOLAP) organized into cubes with dimensions, hierarchies, and measure groups; queries use MDX. A tabular model stores data in the xVelocity in-memory column store (VertiPaq) and is queried with DAX or MDX. Tabular is faster to develop, has better Power BI and Excel integration, scales to very large row counts in memory, and is the strategic direction. Multidimensional remains superior for extremely complex MDX calculations, writeback scenarios, and organizations with deep MDX expertise. New projects almost always choose Tabular.
fs: New project: Tabular (VertiPaq + DAX, Power BI native). Legacy or complex MDX/writeback: Multidimensional.
fj: Multidimensional is always faster because data is pre-aggregated on disk.

followups.q: How does MOLAP pre-aggregation differ from VertiPaq in-memory compression in terms of query performance?
followups.a: MOLAP writes pre-computed aggregates to disk at processing time, so MDX queries that hit an existing aggregation boundary are nearly instant; misses require rolling up from detail. VertiPaq compresses column data into memory with dictionary and run-length encoding, then executes DAX queries in-memory at scan speed without pre-materialized aggregates. VertiPaq is generally faster for ad-hoc slicing because the entire dataset fits in RAM; MOLAP wins where aggregation cost dominates and pre-aggregations align with query patterns.

followups.q: What are writeback cubes in SSAS Multidimensional and why are they rarely used with Tabular?
followups.a: Writeback lets a client write values back into a measure group cell, supporting budgeting and what-if scenarios where planners input figures directly into the cube. SSAS Multidimensional manages a writeback partition for the deltas. Tabular has no native writeback; write-back scenarios use external solutions (Planning Anywhere, BI360, or a custom writeback table plus model refresh). This is a genuine reason some organizations stay on Multidimensional.

q: Explain SSAS Multidimensional partitions: why create them, how are they processed, and what are MOLAP vs ROLAP storage options per partition?
a: A partition divides a measure group into segments, typically by date range (one partition per month or year). Benefits: faster incremental processing (process only the current partition), parallel processing across partitions, and targeted aggregation design per partition. Storage options per partition: MOLAP (compressed aggregates on disk, fastest query), ROLAP (leaves data in relational source, no aggregates, slowest but no processing lag), HOLAP (aggregates in MOLAP, detail in relational). Most production cubes use MOLAP for history and ROLAP for the live/today partition.
fs: Partition by date for incremental processing; MOLAP for history, ROLAP for the live partition.
fj: Partitions are just for performance; they have no impact on query accuracy.

followups.q: How do you automate monthly partition creation and processing in SSAS?
followups.a: Use XMLA scripts (CreatePartition + Process commands) executed via SSMS, SQL Agent job, or SSIS package. The AMO (.NET) or TOM (Tabular OM) libraries let you write C# to programmatically create a new partition for the new month, set its slice expression, set storage to MOLAP, and Process it. SSIS Analysis Services Processing tasks or PowerShell via the AMO assembly are common automation vehicles.

followups.q: What is a partition slice and why must it be set correctly?
followups.a: A slice is the explicit filter SSAS stores as metadata on a partition, declaring which data it contains. At query time SSAS uses slices to skip irrelevant partitions (partition elimination). An incorrect or missing slice causes SSAS to read every partition for every query, negating the performance benefit. An incorrect slice value causes incorrect query results where data from the wrong partition is included or excluded.

q: What is MDX and how does a basic MDX query differ structurally from SQL?
a: MDX (Multidimensional Expressions) queries an SSAS Multidimensional cube by specifying members on axes rather than rows and columns in a flat table. The basic structure is SELECT axis1 ON COLUMNS, axis2 ON ROWS FROM Cube WHERE Slicer. Axes hold sets of tuples (combinations of dimension members); the slicer filters the context. SQL returns flat rows; MDX returns a multidimensional cell set where each intersection is a calculated value driven by the full coordinate context, not just a row filter.
fs: MDX = axes of dimension members + slicer context; SQL = flat row filter + projection.
fj: MDX is just SQL with different keywords for the same row/column data.

code.label: Basic MDX: Sales by Year for US

followups.q: How does MDX evaluation context differ from SQL WHERE clause filtering?
followups.a: MDX evaluation context is multidimensional: every cell value is calculated in the context of the current member on every axis plus the slicer. A measure's value can change depending on the entire coordinate, not just a row filter. Changing the slicer changes all cells. SQL WHERE filters rows before projection; MDX context influences how a calculated measure resolves at each intersection, which is why MDX calculated members can produce results SQL aggregates cannot model.

followups.q: What is the NON EMPTY keyword in MDX and when do you use it?
followups.a: NON EMPTY on an axis removes tuples where all measure cells in that tuple are empty (NULL), suppressing rows or columns with no data. SELECT NON EMPTY {[Measures].[Sales]} ON COLUMNS, NON EMPTY {[Date].[Month].Members} ON ROWS FROM [Sales] suppresses months with zero sales. Without it, the result set includes every calendar member even with no transactions. NON EMPTY can be expensive on large sparse cubes; ensure relevant aggregations exist.

q: How do SSAS Tabular DAX measures, calculated columns, and calculated tables differ in processing timing and cardinality impact?
a: Calculated columns are computed at process/refresh time and stored in VertiPaq compressed storage; they increase cardinality and consume memory proportional to distinct values. Measures are computed at query time in the evaluation context requested by the client; they consume no model storage and scale to billions of rows because they aggregate on demand. Calculated tables are materialized at process time from DAX expressions; they consume memory and re-process when referenced base tables refresh. A common senior mistake is putting logic in a calculated column that should be a measure, bloating model size unnecessarily.
fs: Calculated columns: stored at refresh, increase model size. Measures: evaluated at query time, zero model storage.
fj: Calculated columns and measures are the same; both are just DAX expressions in the model.

followups.q: Why does a high-cardinality calculated column disproportionately hurt VertiPaq compression?
followups.a: VertiPaq compresses via dictionary encoding and run-length encoding. Low-cardinality columns (e.g. status with 5 values) compress to near-nothing. A high-cardinality column like a concatenated ID or a formatted date string has too many distinct values for dictionary compression to help, so it consumes memory close to its raw uncompressed size. This multiplied across millions of rows bloats the model and slows refresh.

followups.q: When is a calculated table preferable to a view in the data source?
followups.a: Use a calculated table when you need to materialize cross-model joins that cannot be expressed in the source (combining two tables from different sources), create role-playing dimension copies without duplicating source data, or generate a date table via CALENDAR() when no date table exists in the warehouse. For transformations expressible in the source SQL, push the logic there so VertiPaq sees a clean, narrow table and the warehouse optimizer handles complexity.

q: How does SSAS Tabular incremental refresh work and what are its prerequisites?
a: Incremental refresh splits a table into range-based partitions automatically based on a RangeStart/RangeEnd parameter pair in the data source query. At refresh time only partitions covering the configured refresh window (e.g. last 30 days) are re-processed; historical partitions remain in MOLAP storage untouched. Prerequisites: the data source must support query folding or parameterized date-range filtering in M, the table must have a datetime column used for the range, and SSAS 2019+ or Power BI Premium/Fabric capacity is required.
fs: Incremental refresh = auto-managed date partitions; only the recent window reprocesses; history stays frozen.
fj: Incremental refresh means only changed rows are detected and updated in place like a CDC.

code.label: Power Query M incremental range filter

followups.q: Why does query folding matter for incremental refresh, and how do you verify it?
followups.a: If query folding is broken, Power Query executes the full table import and applies the date filter client-side, negating the incremental benefit and potentially timing out on large tables. Verify by right-clicking the last applied step in Power Query Editor and checking if 'View Native Query' is enabled (greyed out means no fold). Push all transforms above the date filter to ensure the fold propagates; any non-foldable step inserted before the range filter breaks it.

followups.q: What is the 'detect data changes' option in incremental refresh and how does it reduce processing further?
followups.a: Detect data changes lets you specify a polling column (e.g. a last-modified timestamp max). Before processing the refresh window, SSAS checks if the max value of that column has changed since the last refresh. If unchanged, the partition is skipped entirely. This optimizes for source tables where the refresh window exists but most rows haven't changed since the last run, cutting unnecessary partition reprocessing.

q: How do SSAS Multidimensional dimension hierarchies support MDX navigation, and what are attribute vs user-defined hierarchies?
a: An attribute hierarchy is the default one-level hierarchy SSAS creates for every dimension attribute (All -> Members). A user-defined hierarchy (UDH) stacks multiple attributes into levels (e.g. Year -> Quarter -> Month -> Day) and appears as a navigable drill path in client tools. MDX navigation functions (Descendants, Ancestors, DrilldownMember) rely on these hierarchy metadata to traverse levels. Poorly designed hierarchies with many attribute hierarchies or uneven level populations inflate processing time and storage.
fs: Attribute hierarchy = one attribute as levels; user-defined hierarchy = stacked attributes for drill navigation.
fj: All hierarchies in SSAS are the same; SSAS just creates them automatically from the schema.

followups.q: Why does a ragged hierarchy require special handling in MDX and SSAS?
followups.a: A ragged hierarchy has members at different levels that have no parent at an intermediate level (e.g. a city that belongs directly to a country without a state). SSAS pads missing levels with a copy of the parent to maintain uniform depth for aggregations. MDX navigation that assumes uniform depth can produce incorrect results or return padding members. The HideMemberIf dimension property controls how SSAS hides these pad members from clients.

followups.q: What is the AllMemberName property and why does it matter for MDX total calculations?
followups.a: Every attribute hierarchy has an All Member (default [All]) representing the aggregation across all members of that dimension. A MDX measure evaluated at the All member returns the grand total. Calculated measures that use Parent or Ancestor navigation rely on All being the root. Renaming or disabling AllMember for non-aggregatable dimensions (like account type) requires explicitly handling the root case in MDX, or calculations break at the top level.

q: How do you deploy SSRS reports programmatically using the ReportingServices2010 SOAP API, and what steps are required to publish a report plus its shared data source?
a: Use the ReportService2010.asmx SOAP endpoint (or its REST successor in SSRS 2017+). Steps: (1) Create or verify the target folder with CreateFolder; (2) Upload the shared data source XML with CreateDataSource, supplying a DataSourceDefinition with connection string and credentials type; (3) Upload the RDL bytes with CreateCatalogItem (type='Report', overwrite=true); (4) Call SetItemDataSources to bind the report's data source reference to the shared data source path; (5) Set item-level permissions with SetPolicies if the report requires non-inherited ACLs. Automating this in CI/CD prevents manual deployment errors across dev/staging/prod SSRS instances.
fs: Deploy SSRS via SOAP: CreateFolder → CreateDataSource → CreateCatalogItem → SetItemDataSources → SetPolicies.
fj: You must use Report Manager UI to deploy reports; the SOAP API is read-only and cannot publish new reports.

followups.q: How does the SSRS REST API introduced in SSRS 2017 differ from the legacy SOAP API for deployment automation?
followups.a: The REST API (reportserver/api/v2.0) uses standard HTTP verbs and JSON payloads, making it easier to call from PowerShell, Python, or any HTTP client without a WSDL proxy. Key endpoints: POST /CatalogItems to upload, PATCH to update, DELETE to remove. The REST API covers most deployment operations but still lacks a few SOAP-only features (e.g., linked-report creation, subscription management in some builds), so check the SSRS version before dropping SOAP entirely.

q: What are SSRS linked reports, how do they differ from standard reports, and when would you use them?
a: A linked report is a catalog entry that points to an existing published report (the base report) but stores its own parameter defaults, security, and subscription settings independently. The RDL is shared; only the metadata differs. Use cases: expose the same report to multiple departments with pre-set filters (linked report for Sales has Region=North preloaded, another for West), saving storage and keeping the single RDL as the maintenance target. Linked reports cannot change the report layout or dataset; only parameters and delivery settings differ. Updating the base RDL automatically affects all linked reports.
fs: Linked reports share one RDL but hold independent parameter defaults, ACLs, and subscriptions — one layout, many departmental views.
fj: Linked reports store a full copy of the RDL, so editing one does not affect others.

followups.q: How do you create a linked report via the SSRS SOAP API?
followups.a: Call CreateLinkedItem(ItemPath, Parent, Properties) with the base report path as ItemPath. Pass a Properties array with at minimum the Link property set to the base report's full catalog path. Then call SetItemParameters to set the default parameter overrides specific to this linked report. Unlike CreateCatalogItem, you do not pass RDL bytes — the linked report shares the base definition.

q: Explain SSRS report snapshots and history: what is the difference between execution snapshots and report history snapshots, and how do they affect the ReportServer database?
a: An execution snapshot is a cached, pre-rendered snapshot stored in ReportServerTempDB that serves as the execution source for a report configured to 'Render from snapshot' on a schedule. It replaces live query execution with a stored result, guaranteeing consistent data across sessions and reducing source-system load. A report history snapshot is a permanent point-in-time copy stored in the ReportServer Catalog, accumulating over time (controlled by history retention settings). The history table (dbo.ReportHistory) can grow large; unused history snapshots waste database space and slow backup/restore. Always enforce a retention limit or automate cleanup via ReportService2010.DeleteReportHistorySnapshot.
fs: Execution snapshot = live replacement for consistent delivery; history snapshot = permanent archive — both live in ReportServer DB.
fj: Snapshots are stored in flat files on disk; the database only holds pointers to them.

followups.q: How do execution snapshots interact with SSRS subscription delivery?
followups.a: When a report uses an execution snapshot, subscriptions deliver from that snapshot rather than re-executing the query. All recipients in a standard subscription receive the same snapshot data, which is correct for organization-wide reports but wrong for per-user row-filtered reports. Data-driven subscriptions that rely on User!UserID cannot use a shared execution snapshot; they must execute live or use per-user caching.

q: How do you handle SSRS report performance when a tablix renders more than 100,000 rows, and what architectural alternatives exist?
a: Rendering large row counts in SSRS is inherently slow: the server must materialize all rows in memory before streaming to the client. Strategies: (1) Add parameters to narrow the data (date range, business unit) and make them required; (2) Move aggregations into the SQL query — send grouped data, not raw rows; (3) Use a SSRS drill-through or subreport so the top-level report is a summary and detail is on-demand; (4) Export via a subscription to Excel/CSV on a schedule rather than interactive rendering; (5) For truly massive exports, bypass SSRS rendering entirely — write a SSIS package or a direct SQL → Azure Blob export that runs on the server and emails a download link. SSRS was designed for pixel-perfect paginated output, not bulk data extraction.
fs: For 100k+ rows: parameterize, pre-aggregate in SQL, use drill-through, or push to scheduled export — avoid interactive rendering.
fj: SSRS handles any row count efficiently because it streams rows; memory is never a concern.

followups.q: How does SSRS streaming rendering work for Excel exports vs paginated PDF, and which is faster for large row counts?
followups.a: For Excel XLSX, SSRS renders all report items into memory first, then serializes; there is no true streaming at the application layer. PDF rendering uses a page-by-page pass but still loads the full dataset into the SSRS processing engine. Neither export truly streams from the SQL result set. For large data, the RS.exe command-line utility or the SSRS URL access with rs:Format=EXCELOPENXML can be scripted server-side, but the bottleneck is SSRS in-process memory, not the format. True streaming requires bypassing SSRS and exporting directly from the source.

q: What are SSRS custom assemblies, when are they justified, and what security zone restrictions apply in modern SSRS versions?
a: Custom assemblies are .NET DLLs deployed to the SSRS bin folder that expose static methods callable in RDL expressions, enabling complex business logic not expressible in native expression functions (e.g., proprietary encoding, tax calculation tables, locale-specific formatting). They are justified when inline VB.NET code blocks become unmaintainable and when the logic is reused across many reports. Security: SSRS 2016 and earlier used CAS (Code Access Security) policy files to grant assemblies permissions. In SSRS 2019+ and Power BI Report Server, CAS is deprecated; custom assemblies run fully trusted if placed in the bin folder, removing the need for policy grants but requiring careful code review. Power BI paginated (cloud) does not support custom assemblies at all — a hard migration blocker.
fs: Custom assemblies = .NET DLLs in SSRS bin; fully trusted in 2019+; blocked entirely in Power BI paginated cloud.
fj: Custom assemblies can be deployed as NuGet packages from a private feed; SSRS pulls them automatically.

followups.q: How do you troubleshoot a custom assembly that silently returns null in SSRS report expressions?
followups.a: SSRS swallows exceptions from custom assembly calls in expressions and returns #Error or empty depending on the expression context. Enable SSRS trace logging (RSReportServer.config TraceLevel=Verbose) and check the SSRS log files in the LogFiles folder for ReportProcessingException entries with the assembly method and exception message. Alternatively, wrap the assembly call in an IIF expression to surface the error string: IIF(IsError(Code.MyMethod()), Error(Code.MyMethod()), Code.MyMethod()). Test the assembly independently in a unit test project before deploying to SSRS.

q: How does SSRS Kerberos double-hop authentication work, and what are the common failure modes when SSRS queries a remote SQL Server?
a: When SSRS runs on Server A and queries SQL Server on Server B using Windows integrated security, the service account must be able to delegate the caller's identity to Server B — this is the double-hop. Requirements: the SSRS service account must have Constrained Delegation (or Protocol Transition) configured in Active Directory to SQL Server's SPN; the SQL Server SPN (MSSQLSvc/servername:port) must be correctly registered; and the report data source must use Windows authentication (not stored credentials). Common failures: SPNs missing or duplicated (use setspn -L to verify); service running as LocalSystem (cannot delegate); Kerberos blocked at the network layer (fallback to NTLM, which cannot delegate); or constrained delegation not set for the correct SQL SPN. Symptoms: 'Login failed for user NT AUTHORITY\ANONYMOUS LOGON' in SQL error log.
fs: SSRS double-hop: service account needs AD constrained delegation to SQL SPN; NTLM fallback breaks delegation entirely.
fj: SSRS uses its own token-passing protocol that works over NTLM for remote SQL without any AD configuration.

followups.q: What is the recommended alternative to Kerberos delegation for SSRS when AD configuration is not feasible?
followups.a: Use stored credentials on the SSRS data source: configure the shared data source to use a dedicated service account with a stored username and password. SSRS impersonates this account for the SQL connection rather than delegating the end-user's token, eliminating the double-hop. The trade-off is that row-level security based on User!UserID no longer works — all users share the same SQL connection context. Mitigate with SSRS-layer filtering via a security extension or pass the username as a parameter to a stored procedure that enforces RLS.

q: How do you monitor SSRS health in production, including subscription failure alerting and server-level performance counters?
a: SSRS exposes Windows Performance Monitor counters under 'SQL Server Reporting Services' object: ActiveReportJobs, TotalReportsExecuted, TotalRequests, MemoryUsageBase, and more. Monitor TotalFailedRequests and ScheduledEventsQueue depth for capacity signals. For subscription failures, query ReportServer.dbo.Subscriptions where LastStatus not like 'Mail sent%' or where LastStatus like '%Error%' and LastRunTime < DATEADD(day,-1,GETUTCDATE()). Build a SQL Agent job or Azure Monitor alert on this query. Also monitor the SSRS Windows Event Log (Application) for EventSource='Report Server*' and the SSRS trace log files for ERRORs. In Scale-Out deployments check all nodes: one node's failure causes intermittent user errors that appear non-reproducible.
fs: Monitor SSRS via PerfMon counters + query Subscriptions table LastStatus + SSRS trace log + Event Log.
fj: SSRS has a built-in alerting dashboard; no external monitoring is needed.

followups.q: How does SSRS Scale-Out deployment work and what does it require at the database layer?
followups.a: In Scale-Out, multiple SSRS instances share the same ReportServer and ReportServerTempDB databases and the same encryption key (exported from the first node and imported to all others). A load balancer distributes HTTP traffic. The shared database is the single source of truth for all state: subscriptions, snapshots, caches. The encryption key protects stored credentials; if it is lost or mis-synced across nodes, data source passwords become unreadable. Run rskeymgmt -e to back up the key after every node addition.

q: What is the SSRS URL access protocol, how do you use it to render or export a report from a browser or HTTP client, and what are its security implications?
a: SSRS URL access uses parameterized URLs against the report server endpoint: https://server/reportserver?/FolderPath/ReportName&rs:Format=PDF&rs:Command=Render&ParameterName=Value. Key prefixes: rs: for server directives (Format, Command, Snapshot), rc: for rendering control (Toolbar, Parameters visibility), and bare names for report parameters. Formats: PDF, EXCEL, EXCELOPENXML, CSV, XML, IMAGE. Security implication: any user who can guess a valid URL and has Browser role can render or export sensitive reports directly, bypassing portal navigation. Mitigate by requiring authentication (disable anonymous access), using HTTPS, and applying item-level permissions. In Power BI paginated, URL access is replaced by embed tokens and REST API, so URL access knowledge is SSRS-specific.
fs: SSRS URL access: ?/path&rs:Format=PDF&param=val — powerful but requires tight ACLs since any guessable URL renders data.
fj: SSRS URL access only works inside the Report Manager portal; it cannot be called from an external application.

followups.q: How do you embed an SSRS report in a custom web application without displaying the SSRS toolbar?
followups.a: Use URL access with rc:Toolbar=false to hide the toolbar and rc:Parameters=false to hide the parameter panel, then embed the URL in an <iframe>. For forms-authenticated SSRS, you must handle authentication before the iframe loads — either use Windows integrated auth (the iframe inherits the browser session) or implement a ReportViewer control (.NET) that handles the auth handshake. For modern stacks, the ReportViewer WebForms/MVC control (Microsoft.Reporting.WebForms) or the JavaScript ReportingServices library provides cleaner integration than raw iframes.

q: How does SSAS Tabular use VertiPaq compression and columnar storage, and what column characteristics maximize compression ratios?
a: VertiPaq stores each column independently as a column store, applying value encoding (integer dictionary mapping) and run-length encoding (RLE) on sorted sequences. Compression is highest when cardinality is low and values repeat in long runs. Columns with high cardinality (GUIDs, free-text, timestamps at millisecond precision) compress poorly and expand memory footprint. To optimize: reduce cardinality by truncating timestamps to day/hour, replacing GUIDs with integer surrogate keys, and removing columns not used in any measure or relationship.
